question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the timing window between inner.ServeHTTP(tracingResponseWriter, request) completing and the log.Info(message) call to submit a tampered transaction that is accepted by the network but whose log entry is never written due to a concurrent server shutdown, leaving no audit record of the tampered submission?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.Write()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where the inner handler for /construction/submit writes a success response body and then writes an error body in a second Write() call, causing tracingResponseWriter to store only the error body in w.data while the HTTP response already sent the success body, creating an inconsistency between the logged response and the actual transaction submission outcome?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user omit both X-Real-IP and X-Forwarded-For headers and spoof the TCP source address (via a MITM or transparent proxy) so that net.SplitHostPort(r.RemoteAddr) returns an attacker-controlled IP, causing TracingMiddleware to log a forged source IP for a tampered /construction/submit request?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a Unicode right-to-left override character (U+202E) in the URL path, causing TracingMiddleware's log output to visually reverse the displayed path in log viewers, making a tampered transaction submission path appear as a benign internal path to human reviewers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request body size limits in TracingMiddleware to send a /construction/submit request with a multi-gigabyte body containing a tampered transaction, causing the server to buffer the entire body in memory before inner.ServeHTTP processes it, leading to OOM and service crash that prevents legitimate transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a path containing format string directives (e.g., '%s', '%d', '%v') that are passed into fmt.Sprintf via the path variable, and if any future code change passes path directly as a format string rather than as an argument, cause a format string vulnerability that leaks memory or crashes the server during tampered transaction logging?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set X-Forwarded-For to an IPv6 address with embedded colons (e.g., '::ffff:192.0.2.1') that is logged verbatim by TracingMiddleware, causing log parsers that split on colons to misparse the log entry for a tampered /construction/submit request and fail to extract the correct source IP for alerting?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware does not add any request ID or correlation token to the request context before calling inner.ServeHTTP, making it impossible to correlate a tampered /construction/submit request's log entry with downstream service logs, enabling the attacker to deny having submitted a specific tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a path that is exactly 0 bytes (empty string), causing internalPaths[path] to return false (empty string is not in the map) and log.Info to be called with a message containing an empty path field, breaking log-based alerting rules that match on '/construction/submit' path patterns for tampered transaction detection?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any HMAC or cryptographic integrity check in TracingMiddleware on the request body to submit a transaction where the hex-encoded signed transaction bytes have been modified after signing, relying on the middleware to pass the tampered bytes through to inner.ServeHTTP without any verification?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a Content-Type header that causes the inner handler to misparse the transaction body, while TracingMiddleware logs only the path and status code — not the Content-Type — making the content-type-based tampering invisible in the audit log?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set X-Real-IP to a value containing spaces (e.g., '1.2.3.4 5.6.7.8'), which passes the len(ipAddress) == 0 check, causing TracingMiddleware to log a malformed IP string for a tampered /construction/submit request that breaks IP-based log analysis tools?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware wraps the ResponseWriter but does not wrap the request body reader, allowing a tampered transaction body to be read and forwarded to inner.ServeHTTP without any middleware-level inspection, making TracingMiddleware a transparent pass-through for transaction tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request that causes inner.ServeHTTP to panic, and if the panic is recovered by a higher-level middleware, TracingMiddleware will still log the request with the default statusCode of 200 (set in newTracingResponseWriter), making a failed tampered transaction submission appear successful in the audit log?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a path containing the string 'level=info' or 'level=error', causing TracingMiddleware's log output to contain duplicate level fields that confuse structured log parsers and cause the tampered transaction submission event to be miscategorized or dropped?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set X-Forwarded-For to a value exceeding the maximum log line length of the logging backend, causing TracingMiddleware's log entry for a tampered /construction/submit request to be truncated, dropping the path and status code fields that would identify the tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware does not validate that the HTTP method is POST for /construction/submit, allowing them to send a tampered transaction via a PATCH or OPTIONS request that bypasses method-specific WAF rules while TracingMiddleware logs the non-standard method without raising an alert?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.WriteHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where the inner handler calls WriteHeader(200) for a tampered transaction that was accepted, and then a deferred function calls WriteHeader(500) after an error, causing tracingResponseWriter to log HTTP 500 for a transaction that was actually submitted to the network, creating a false negative in tampered transaction detection?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a path containing the exact text of a legitimate log entry (e.g., '10.0.0.1 POST /construction/submit (200) in 5ms'), causing TracingMiddleware to emit a log line that is indistinguishable from a real transaction submission log, forging evidence of a legitimate transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential execution of inner.ServeHTTP followed by log.Info in TracingMiddleware to submit a tampered transaction and then immediately send a flood of requests that fill the log buffer, causing the log entry for the tampered submission to be evicted from an in-memory log buffer before it is persisted to disk?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set both X-Real-IP and X-Forwarded-For to empty strings, forcing TracingMiddleware to fall back to net.SplitHostPort(r.RemoteAddr), and if RemoteAddr is also empty or malformed, net.SplitHostPort returns an empty string, causing TracingMiddleware to log a blank IP for a tampered /construction/submit request?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a path that contains the string 'time=' followed by a fake duration value, causing TracingMiddleware's fmt.Sprintf log message to contain two 'in X' fields (the injected one and the real time.Since(start)), confusing log parsers that extract timing data for anomaly detection of tampered transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware does not set any response headers (e.g., X-Request-ID) that would allow clients to correlate their tampered /construction/submit request with a specific server-side log entry, enabling the attacker to plausibly deny which specific request contained the tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /construction/submit request with a path containing a null byte (%00), which request.URL.RequestURI() may return as-is, causing TracingMiddleware's log message to be truncated at the null byte in C-based log processing tools, hiding the status code and timing fields for the tampered transaction submission?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set X-Forwarded-For to a value containing a colon (e.g., 'attacker:8080'), causing TracingMiddleware to log a host:port string as the client IP for a tampered /construction/submit request, breaking IP-based geolocation and threat intelligence lookups used to detect tampered transaction sources?
